key_insight: A multivariate active learning strategy can build one polynomial chaos surrogate that efficiently captures multiple engineering quantities of interest while using fewer high-cost simulations.
engineering_relevance: This is useful for finite-element-based design and uncertainty quantification when several responses must be predicted consistently from the same uncertain inputs.
method: Start with a candidate pool and fit polynomial chaos expansion surrogates for vector-valued outputs.
Sequentially add samples that maximize local contribution to output variance across all QoIs.
Balance exploration of the input space with exploitation of aggregated variance information to improve sampling efficiency.
evidence_signal: Compared with non-sequential Latin Hypercube Sampling on several engineering examples, the proposed approach improved surrogate accuracy, stability, and estimation of second-order statistics.
watch_item: Performance depends on the candidate pool and the balance between exploration and variance-driven selection, so poorly chosen settings may reduce efficiency or miss output-specific sensitivities.
paper_title: Uncertainty Quantification of Engineering Structures by Polynomial Chaos Expansion and Multivariate Active Learning
